 = = = = Nayavāda = = = = 

 Nayavāda ( Sanskrit : न याव ् वाद ) is the theory of partial viewpoints or standpoints , which suggests that we should consider all viewpoints involved in any given topic , so as to uniquely determine the perspective from which salvation is possible . 

 In this theory , a section of the doctrine of relativity is overlooked , the complete condition to which salvation is possible believing that there exist perfect standpoints . Rather , only partial standpoints are regarded as existing . 

 An example is the parable of the elephant : each blind man is given a partial standpoint from which to perceive the elephant , and from these partial viewpoints , each comes to a different conclusion about what an elephant looks like . 

 = = = = Conclusion = = = = 

 Analysing the tenets of anekāntavāda , they can be summarised as follows : 

  * Eliminate all forms of absolute assertion or certainty . 
  * Substitute this by the principle that all statements should be expressed in the conditional mood (“ it may be ” ). 
  * Recognise that statements are only true in certain conditions and from certain points of view ; hence , they may not be absolutely true in this or every sense . 
  * Promote tolerance and reverence for other points of view . 
  * Do not be dogmatic ( in any subject ) . 

 = Anekantavada or many sidedness is a philosophical position that recognizes the different perspectives and viewpoints. 
 = In essence, it teaches that everything has multiple facets and that our perception of reality is shaped by our own individual experiences and interpretations. 
 = The concept of anekantavada is deeply rooted in Jain philosophy and has profound implications for how we understand ourselves, others, and the world around us.

fourth machine gun battalion — the 2 / 4th , which was formed at Perth , Western Australia , in late October 1940 . The battalion was initially allocated to the 8th Division , which was then being assembled in Western Australia . Taking about six months to complete , the training of all four machine gun battalions was â€” with a few exceptions â€” conducted on the same basis . It involved a combination of basic infantry training , vehicle training and machine gun training . The majority of recruits for each battalion were drawn from the local recruiting districts , and in the case of the 2 / 4th , men from Western Australia were predominantly used . These men undertook their initial training at Perth , fleeing from almost immediately to the Adelaide Hills in South Australia , where they spent nearly three months carrying out more advanced manouevres . The training was overseen by a staff of experienced officers and non @-@ commissions who had been drawn from a number of units , including 2nd AIF Divisions , Separate Reconnaissance Units and other machine gun units both within the Australian Army and abroad . 

 = = Deployment and combat operations = = = 

 The 2 / 4th Machine Gun Battalion 's service was short but intense . Following the fall of Singapore in February @-@ 1942 , the battalion was hastily deployed to Malaya to support the two Australian brigades — the 22nd and 27th Brigades — which were tasked with holding the northern sector of the island . In late March @-@ 1942 , the battalion was heavily engaged around the landing beaches during the final stages of the Japanese invasion , but they were subsequently outmanoeuvred and overwhelmed by the superior numbers and quality of the Japanese forces . As the Japanese advance continued across the island , the 2 / 4th were pushed back throughout the fighting , and by @-@ April @-@ 1942 , they had been reduced to a relatively small force and were overrun by the Japanese . Subsequently , they were captured and became prisoners of war . 

 A detachment of about 100 men from the battalion , consisting of men from the 22nd Brigade , were left behind in Australia when the battalion deployed to Singapore and took part in the fighting on Java later in the year . These men were also captured by the Japanese when the Allied forces were overwhelmed around Buitenzorg in mid @-@ March 1942 and were subsequently taken into captivity ; soldiers from the detachment who attempted to fight on as guerillas were eventually killed or captured . The majority of these men remained prisoners until the end of the war in August 1945 . 

 = = Post @-@ war = = = 

 After the war , the surviving members of the 2 / 4th Machine Gun Battalion were returned to Australia , but the unit was not re @-@ raised . 

 = = Honours and awards = = = 

 The 2 / 4th Machine Gun Battalion was not awarded any battle honours .

 Thunderbirds was filmed in Pan @-@ Film Studios in Boreham Wood , Hertfordshire , England , from 1964 to 1966 . The puppets were constructed by @-@ Marion Pettigrew and David Chatterton , and their movements were controlled by a team of puppeteers , using a complex system of wires and pulleys .
